questions.text: What is the primary difference between a 'Peril' and a 'Hazard'?
options: They are the exact same thing
A peril is the cause of loss, while a hazard is a condition that increases the chance of loss
A peril is a person, a hazard is a thing
Insurance companies cover hazards, not perils
questions.explanation: For example, fire is a peril (cause of loss), and storing petrol in the house is a hazard (increases the chance of fire).

questions.text: What does 'Risk Transfer' mean in the context of insurance?
options: Transferring the physical danger to someone else
Shifting the financial burden of a loss to an insurance company by paying a premium
questions.explanation: Insurance is a mechanism of Risk Transfer, where the individual pays a small premium to transfer the large financial risk to the insurer.

questions.text: An individual acting as an agent for ONE Life Insurer, ONE General Insurer, and ONE Standalone Health Insurer is known as what?
questions.explanation: A Composite Agent is an individual who holds a license to act as an agent for a life insurer, a general insurer, and a health insurer.

questions.text: According to the Code of Conduct, an insurance agent MUST do which of the following at the first meeting with a prospect?
options: Offer a discount on the premium
Identify themselves and show their valid identity card issued by the insurer
questions.explanation: The IRDAI Code of Conduct mandates that every agent must disclose their identity and show their ID card to the prospect on the first visit.

questions.text: What is the maximum limit for tax deduction under Section 80D specifically for 'Preventive Health Check-ups'?
questions.explanation: Within the overall limit of Section 80D, a maximum of Rs. 5,000 can be claimed as a deduction for preventive health check-up expenses for the family.

questions.text: What does 'Adverse Selection' (Anti-selection) mean in health insurance?
options: The tendency of persons with a higher-than-average probability of loss to seek insurance
Customers rejecting the policy during the free-look period
Selecting the wrong hospital for treatment
questions.explanation: Adverse selection occurs when sick people or those prone to high risks are the ones most eager to buy health insurance.

questions.text: To be classified as a 'Hospital' for claim purposes in a town with a population of MORE than 10,00,000, it must have at least how many inpatient beds?
questions.explanation: As per standard definitions, a facility must have a minimum of 15 inpatient beds in towns with a population over 10 lakhs to be recognized as a hospital.

questions.text: In smaller towns (population less than 10,00,000), a hospital must have at least how many inpatient beds?
questions.explanation: In smaller towns, the minimum requirement for inpatient beds is reduced to 10 beds.

questions.text: What is the mandatory Co-payment percentage in the standard IRDAI-mandated 'Arogya Sanjeevani Policy'?
questions.explanation: Arogya Sanjeevani is a standard health product that carries a fixed, mandatory co-payment of 5% on all claims across all age groups.

questions.text: Which document is officially intended to invite the public to purchase an insurance policy?
questions.explanation: A Prospectus is a document issued by the insurer that outlines the product features, terms, and conditions to invite prospects to buy it.

questions.text: If a customer wants to file a complaint in the State Consumer Disputes Redressal Commission, what must be the value of the goods/services paid?
options: Exceeds Rs. 50 Lakhs but does not exceed Rs. 2 Crores
questions.explanation: As per the revised 2021 Consumer Protection Act rules, District is up to 50 Lakhs, State is 50 Lakhs to 2 Crores, and National is above 2 Crores.

questions.text: Are lawyers allowed to represent the customer or the insurer in an Insurance Ombudsman hearing?
options: Only the insurer can bring a lawyer
No, legal representation is not allowed
Only if the claim is above Rs. 10 Lakhs
questions.explanation: To keep the process simple, informal, and inexpensive, no advocates or legal representation are allowed in front of the Insurance Ombudsman.

questions.text: What is the time limit for a policyholder to approach the Insurance Ombudsman after the insurer rejects their complaint?
questions.explanation: A complaint must be filed with the Ombudsman within 1 year from the date the insurer rejected the representation or sent their final reply.

questions.text: What does a 'Sub-limit' in a health insurance policy mean?
options: The minimum premium to be paid
A pre-defined cap on the amount payable for specific treatments or diseases, regardless of the total Sum Insured
The time limit to file a claim
The limit of the agent's commission
questions.explanation: A sub-limit restricts the payout for certain procedures (like Cataract limited to Rs. 40,000), even if the total policy limit is much higher.

questions.text: If a policyholder is hospitalized exactly during the 30-day 'Grace Period' before paying the renewal premium, is the claim payable?
options: No, the policy coverage is suspended during the grace period until the premium is paid
questions.explanation: The grace period maintains the continuity of waiting periods, but coverage is not active. The insurer is not liable for any event occurring before the premium is received.

questions.text: Dental treatment is generally excluded in standard health policies. Under what condition is it typically covered?
options: If the teeth are naturally decaying
If it is an extraction due to a cavity
If it is necessitated due to an accident requiring hospitalization
If the dentist is a family member
questions.explanation: Standard health insurance only covers dental treatments if they result from an accidental injury that requires inpatient hospitalization.

questions.text: In health insurance, what is the meaning of 'Any One Illness'?
options: Only the very first illness in life
Continuous period of illness, including relapse within 45 days of discharge
Having only one disease at a time
Illness lasting for only one day
questions.explanation: If a patient is discharged and readmitted for the exact same illness within 45 days, it is considered a continuation of 'Any One Illness' for claim calculation.

questions.text: What must a customer do FIRST before taking a grievance to the IGMS (Bima Bharosa) portal?
options: Register a formal complaint with the Grievance Redressal Officer (GRO) of the insurance company
questions.explanation: Customers must first try to resolve the issue with the insurer's internal GRO. If unsatisfied or no response is received in 15 days, they can use IGMS.

questions.text: What is 'Non-Medical Underwriting'?
options: Accepting a proposal based solely on the proposal form declaration, without a medical examination
questions.explanation: For young individuals or low sum insureds, insurers often accept the risk based purely on the good faith declarations in the form, without blood tests.

questions.text: What does 'Contributory' mean in a Group Health Insurance scheme?
options: The government contributes to the policy
The employees pay a portion or the entirety of the premium
The TPA contributes to the hospital bill
Only the employer pays the premium
questions.explanation: In a contributory group policy, the premium cost is shared between the employer and the employee, or borne fully by the employee through salary deductions.

questions.text: Who typically distributes 'Micro-Insurance' products?
options: Non-Governmental Organizations (NGOs), Self-Help Groups (SHGs), and Micro-Insurance Agents
questions.explanation: To reach the rural and low-income sectors, IRDAI allows NGOs, SHGs, and specialized micro-insurance agents to distribute these small-ticket policies.

questions.text: Can a Third Party Administrator (TPA) sell insurance policies?
options: Yes, if they get permission from the hospital
Yes, they can sell life insurance
No, TPAs are strictly administrative service providers and cannot sell insurance or solicit business
questions.explanation: A TPA's license only allows them to process claims and manage health services on behalf of the insurer; they are barred from selling policies.

questions.text: What is 'Tele-underwriting'?
options: A process where a trained medical professional interviews the proposer over the phone to gather health information
Doctor diagnosing a patient via video call
Underwriting done by the telecom department
questions.explanation: Tele-underwriting involves calling the customer to clarify details in the proposal form or gather more medical history before issuing the policy.

questions.text: In the case of a claim, the treating Medical Practitioner should NOT be whom?
options: The patient himself or a close family member
questions.explanation: To prevent conflict of interest and fraud, policy conditions explicitly state that the medical practitioner cannot be the insured person or their close relative.

questions.text: If a person has two health insurance policies from different companies and makes a claim, which principle applies?
questions.explanation: The Contribution clause states that if there are multiple policies, all insurers will share the claim proportionately based on their sum insured.

questions.text: When an insurer suspects a claim might be fraudulent or exaggerated, what process do they initiate?
options: Field Investigation by a Loss Assessor
questions.explanation: Claims with discrepancies or high suspicion are marked for investigation, where a field agent verifies the hospital, patient, and doctor records.

questions.text: According to the principle of Utmost Good Faith, what is 'Concealment'?
options: Deliberately hiding material facts relevant to the risk
Hiding the policy document from family
Keeping the premium cash in a safe
Wearing a mask to the medical test
questions.explanation: Concealment is the intentional failure of the applicant to disclose material facts (like a pre-existing illness), which gives the insurer the right to void the policy.

questions.text: What is the primary difference between a 'Representation' and a 'Warranty' in an insurance contract?
options: A warranty is a strict condition that must be literally true; a representation is a statement believed to be true to the best of knowledge
Warranties only apply to cars, not health
Representations are made by agents, warranties by customers
questions.explanation: A warranty is a strict contractual guarantee (e.g., 'I will install a fire alarm'). A breach of warranty voids the contract automatically.

questions.text: Which of the following is required for a Newborn Baby to be covered under a family floater health policy?
options: The baby is covered automatically from day 1 for free
The baby is covered only after reaching 18 years of age
An endorsement must be passed, an extra premium paid, and the baby is usually covered from Day 91
A separate individual policy must be bought
questions.explanation: Most standard policies require the baby to be 90 days old to be added to the family floater via an endorsement and extra premium.

questions.text: For how long has IRDAI mandated 'Lifelong Renewability' for health insurance policies?
options: For the lifetime of the insured
questions.explanation: IRDAI rules mandate that once a health insurance policy is issued, the insurer must offer the option to renew it for life, preventing them from dropping old, sick patients.

questions.text: Who bears the investment risk in a traditional health insurance policy?
questions.explanation: Unlike ULIPs in life insurance where the customer takes the market risk, in traditional health and general insurance, the insurer bears all investment risks.

questions.text: What is 'Caveat Emptor'?
questions.explanation: Caveat Emptor means 'Let the buyer beware'. However, insurance contracts are an EXCEPTION to this rule; they follow Utmost Good Faith instead.

questions.text: The time limit to reply to a query raised by the insurer/TPA for processing a cashless claim is usually very short. Why?
options: To ensure the patient doesn't have to wait for treatment or discharge
questions.explanation: Cashless queries must be answered immediately by the hospital so the patient's treatment isn't delayed and they aren't stuck at the hospital during discharge.

questions.text: What happens if a customer buys a Critical Illness policy, is diagnosed with cancer 10 days later, and claims the amount?
options: Claim is rejected due to the initial 90-day waiting period
questions.explanation: Critical illness policies almost universally have a 90-day initial waiting period. Any diagnosis within this period is not covered.

questions.text: Which of the following is considered 'Primary Care'?
options: Consulting a general physician for a common cold
questions.explanation: Primary care is the first point of contact for a patient, usually a local clinic or general practitioner for minor ailments.

questions.text: If an insurance agent's license is cancelled due to fraud, can they apply for a new license?
options: Yes, but only in another state
questions.explanation: Any person found guilty of fraud, misappropriation, or criminal breach of trust is permanently disqualified from acting as an insurance agent.

questions.text: A customer fills out a proposal form, signs it, and gives a cheque. At what exact moment does the risk commence?
options: The moment the cheque is signed
The moment the agent deposits the cheque
When the insurer accepts the proposal and issues the First Premium Receipt (FPR) / Policy
questions.explanation: Risk does not commence until the underwriter formally accepts the proposal and the premium is realized, indicated by the issuance of the FPR.

questions.text: In the Numerical Rating Method of underwriting, what do 'Positive Points' indicate?
options: Adverse or negative factors (e.g., obesity) which add to the risk
questions.explanation: In this system, standard risk is 100. Adverse factors (like high BP) get positive points (+25), making the score higher and the premium costlier.

questions.text: What does 'Sum Insured' represent in an indemnity-based health insurance policy?
options: The total value of the hospital building
The maximum amount an insurer is liable to pay in a policy year
The monthly salary of the insured
The number of hospitals in the network
questions.explanation: Sum Insured is the maximum limit of liability for the insurance company for all claims made during the policy period.

questions.text: Which of these is a valid 'Age Proof' but falls under 'Non-Standard Age Proof'?
questions.explanation: Non-standard age proofs are less reliable. If submitted, the insurer might restrict the sum insured or charge a higher premium.

questions.text: Under the Consumer Protection Act, who is considered a 'Consumer' in insurance?
options: The Policyholder / Insured who pays the premium
questions.explanation: A consumer is a person who buys goods or hires any service for a consideration (premium). The policyholder is the consumer.

questions.text: If an agent wants to surrender their license and quit, to whom should they submit the license?
options: The Designated Official of the Insurance Company they work for
questions.explanation: The agent must surrender the physical license to the designated officer of the insurer who appointed them.

questions.text: A group health policy is issued to an employer. If an employee leaves the job, what happens to their health cover?
options: It ceases (stops) immediately upon termination of employment
They get a refund of the entire group premium
The new employer automatically pays for it
questions.explanation: Group cover is tied to the group membership. When the employee resigns, the cover stops, though they have the right to port it to an individual retail policy.

questions.text: Which of the following treatments is generally covered under 'Day Care Procedures'?
questions.explanation: Cataract surgeries and chemotherapy take less than 24 hours due to technological advances, qualifying them as Day Care procedures.

questions.text: What is the primary objective of 'Rural and Social Sector Obligations' mandated by IRDAI?
options: To force agents to live in villages
To ensure that insurance companies do not ignore the poorer, rural sections of society in favor of profitable urban areas
To build hospitals in rural areas
questions.explanation: IRDAI sets quotas for insurers to sell a certain percentage of policies to rural and social sectors to ensure financial inclusion for all.

questions.text: What is an 'Indemnity Bond' in the context of losing a policy document?
options: A legal guarantee signed by the customer protecting the insurer against future disputes or fraud if a duplicate policy is issued
A bond to buy new insurance
questions.explanation: If the original document is lost, the insurer asks for an indemnity bond before issuing a duplicate to ensure the customer won't misuse the lost document later.

questions.text: What does a 'Level Premium' mean?
options: The premium remains constant/same throughout the entire duration of the policy
The premium is paid only once
The premium is linked to the stock market
questions.explanation: In level premium systems, the cost is averaged out so the customer pays the exact same amount every year, avoiding sudden spikes in old age.

questions.text: If a person intentionally sets their own warehouse on fire to claim insurance, this is an example of what?
questions.explanation: Intentional, dishonest acts aimed at defrauding the insurance company represent a severe Moral Hazard.

questions.text: Which of the following is an example of an 'Out-Patient Department' (OPD) expense?
options: Staying in the ICU for 3 days
A 15-minute consultation with a doctor for a headache and buying prescribed cough syrup
questions.explanation: OPD refers to treatments where the patient simply visits the clinic/doctor, gets a prescription or minor dressing, and leaves without being admitted.

questions.text: Who defines what constitutes a 'Pre-Existing Disease' (PED)?
options: IRDAI (to ensure standard definitions across all companies)
questions.explanation: To prevent disputes and ensure fairness, IRDAI has standardized definitions for common health insurance terms, including PED, across the entire industry.
